ex.u:
Mazhar Imam, Aksar Yaad Aate Hain; Jigar Moradabadi:
ایک دفعہ حفیظؔ جالندھری کو چٹگاؤں میں پچیس ہزار روپے کی تھیلی پیش کی گئی تھی۔
ek dafā’ hafīz jālandharī ko caṭgāõ mẽ paccīs hazār rupye kī thailī peś kī gaī thī.
Once, Hafiz Jalandhari was offered in Chittagong a bag worth twenty-five thousand rupees.

چچی.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀘𑀸𑀘𑁆𑀘 (*cācca) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from onomatopoeic nursery words.

چراغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چراغ (čirâġ, čarâġ).

senses.g: A domestic spinning wheel, used mostly for spinning cotton.

چرخہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چَرْخَه (čarxa). Doublet of چاک (cāk) and چکر (cakkar, cakra).

چرندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چرنده (čaranda), from چریدن (čarīdan, “to graze”).

ex.u: اب پچھتائے کیا ہووت جب چڑیا چگ گئی کھیت
ex.r: ab pachtāe kyā hūvat jab ciṛiyā cug gaī khēt
ex.e: What good is it to regret when the bird has already pecked through the crops?

چڑیا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀘𑀺𑀟𑀺𑀕𑀸 (*ciḍigā), feminine form of 𑀘𑀺𑀟𑀺𑀕 (ciḍiga), from Sanskrit *चिटिक (*ciṭika), from चटक (caṭaka, “sparrow”).

چڑیل.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀘𑀼𑀟 (*cuḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀮𑁆𑀮- (-illa-). Cognate with Gujarati ચૂડેલ (cūḍel). Related to چُوت (cūt, “cunt”)، چُڑ (cuṛ, “vulva”)، چُڈّو (cuḍḍo, “harlot”).

senses.g: to make one get on, board (a mode of transportation)
to cook (ie. on a fire/barbecue etc.)
Chishtian (a city in Bahawalnagar district, Punjab, Pakistan)

چشمک.e: From Classical Persian چشمک (čašmak, literally “a small eye”).

senses.g: Chagatai; a now-extinct Turkic language closely related to Uzbek and Uyghur, characterized by its advanced literary tradition and prestigious status and heavily Persianized form, initially spoken by the Mughals whence the many Chagatai loanwords preserved in Urdu
of or pertaining to Chagatai-speakers or Chagatai people
of or pertaining to Chagatai Khan, Genghis Khan's second son; or his Chagatai Khanate, a Turkicized khanate of Central Asia

چغتائی.e: From Chagatai جغتای (jağatāy), compare Uyghur چاغاتاي (chaghatay) and Persian جغتایی (jaġatâyi). Also known more archaically as تُرکی (Turkī, “Turkic; Chagatai”); what the first Mughal Emperor Babur referred to his language as in his autobiography, the Baburnama.

senses.g: fool; idiot; a highly gullible person

چقماق.e: Borrowed from Chagatai چقمق (čqmq). Compare Turkish çakmak.

چقمق.e: Borrowed from Chagatai چقمق (čqmq). Compare Turkish çakmak.

چکت.e: Learned borrowing from Classical Sanskrit चकित (cakita, “trembling; (by extension) frightened, startled, shocked”). By surface analysis, چَکْ (cak, stem of چَکْنا (caknā, “to be surprised, bewildered or amazed”)) + ـِت (-it, “-ed”).

چکر.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit चक्र (cakra). Doublet of چرخہ (carxa), چاک (cāk), چکر (cakr), and چَکَّہ (cakka).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit चक्र (cakra). Doublet of چرخہ (carxā), چاک (cāk), and چکر (cakkar).

senses.g: chakravartin, wheel-turner (in reference to the dharmachakra), world-ruler, upholder of dharma, a title taken by Indian emperors

چکمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چکمه (čakma), from Chagatai چكمه (čäkmä) or Azerbaijani çəkmə.

چکنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀘𑀺𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀡 (cikkaṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit चिक्कण (cikkaṇa).

senses.g: to be paid off, repaid (e.g. a debt)

چکنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀘𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀇 (cukkaï), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀘𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀢𑀺 (*cukkati).

senses.g: to be astonished, bewildered, surprised, or amazed

چکنا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *चक्यते (*cakyate, “to tremble (out of fear, astonishment, bewilderment, etc.)”). Related to چَکِت (cakit).

senses.g: alternative form of چَکْھنا (cakhnā, “to experience; taste; suffer; enjoy”)

چکنا.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

senses.g: Chakwal, a city in Punjab, Pakistan. It is the capital of Chakwal District

چکھنا.e: Urdu verb set چَکْھنا (cakhnā) چَکھانا (cakhānā) Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀤𑀺 (cakkhadi), possibly from Sanskrit *चक्षति (*cakṣati, “tastes”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan. Cognate with Assamese চাকা (saka) / চকা (soka).

senses.g: of what kind, what manner, what state
Chilas (a city in Diamer district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)
to cause to walk; to move
to scream, to screech, to shriek
to cry out, to make an outcry, to shout, to exclaim aloud, to yell

چلانا.e: From Sanskrit चिल्ल (cilla) (kite (bird)) + آ (ā, causative augment) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

چلنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀮𑀤𑀺 (caladi), from Sanskrit चलति (calati), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *čálHati, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *čálHati, from Proto-Indo-European *kʷél-e-ti, from *kʷel- (“to move; to turn (around)”). Compare Persian چلیدن (čalīdan, “to go; to walk”), Persian چالاک (čâlâk, “fast, agile, clever”).

چلی.e: Borrowed from English Chile, from Spanish Chile.

senses.g: a person from low class, low-caste

چمبیلی.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *चम्पवेल्लि (*campavelli). Cognate with Gujarati ચંબેલી (cambelī), Marathi चमेली (camelī), Punjabi چَمبیلی / ਚੰਬੇਲੀ (cambelī).

چمچ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چمچه (čamča, čumča), from Turkic. Compare Sylheti ꠌꠣꠝꠌ (samoso).

چمچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چَمْچَه (čamča), from Turkic.

چمڑا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀫𑁆𑀫 (camma) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-) + -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit चर्मन् (cárman), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *ćárma, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *čárma.

چمک.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀫𑀓𑁆𑀓 (camakka), perhaps from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀘𑀫𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*camakka).

چمکنا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀘𑀫𑁆𑀫𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*cammakka), from an onomatopoeic 𑀘𑀫𑁆𑀫 (camma) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-). Related to چونکنا (cauṅknā). Cognate with Punjabi ਚਮਕਣਾ (camkaṇā) / چَمَکنا (camaknā), Gujarati ચમકવું (camakvũ), Marathi चमकणे (ċamakṇe).

senses.g: Chaman (a city in Qilla Abdullah district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
an army or division of an army (129 elephants, as many cars, 2187 horse, and 3645 foot)
Chenab (a river in Punjab, Pakistan and the union territory of Jammu and Kashmir and the state of Himachal Pradesh in India)

چناب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چَنَاب (čanāb). Compare Punjabi جَھناں (jhanāṉ) / چَنھاں (canhāṉ) / ਝਨਾਂ (jhanā̃) and Saraiki چَنان٘ہہ (canāṉh).

senses.g: Rabwah, Chenab Nagar (a city in Chiniot district, Pakistan)

چناب نگر.e: From چناب (canāb, “Chenab”) + نگر (nagar, “city; town”), literally “the city of Chenab”.

چندر.e: From Sanskrit चन्द्र (candrá). Doublet of چاند (cā̃d).

senses.g: candrabindu, the "moon dot" nasal vowel marker of the Devanagari script
sandalwood (the aromatic heartwood of sandalwood trees)
sandalwood (any of various tropical trees of the genus Santalum)

چندن.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit चन्दन (candana). Doublet of صَنْدَل (sandal).

senses.g: Chandigarh (a city and union territory in northern India, located between Punjab and Haryana; the capital city of Haryana and Punjab)
claw (of a hunting animal); talon (of a bird)

چننا.e: Inherited from Prakrit चिणेइ (ciṇei), from Sanskrit चिनोति (cinoti). Doublet of چُنْنَا (cunnā) and related to بِینْنا (bīnnā, “to pluck”).

Inherited from Prakrit चुणइ (cuṇaï, “pecks up”), a variant of चिणेइ (ciṇei, “to gather, collect”) perhaps from contamination with गुहइ (guhaï, “strings together”). Ultimately from Sanskrit चिनोति (cinoti, “arranges”). Doublet of چِنْنَا (cinnā). Cognate with Gujarati ચુણવું (cuṇvũ) and Nepali चुन्नु (cunnu).

senses.g: Chiniot (a city in Chiniot district, Punjab, Pakistan)

چنیوٹ.e: Named after an ancient king's daughter named Chandan + Sanskrit कोट (koṭa, “fort”).

senses.g: Chennai (the capital and largest city of Tamil Nadu, India)
Chennai (a district of Tamil Nadu, India, containing most of metro Chennai)

چوبے.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀘𑀉𑀯𑁂𑀤 (caüveda), 𑀘𑀉𑀯𑁂𑀬 (caüveya), 𑀘𑀉𑀯𑁆𑀯𑁂𑀤 (caüvveda) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit चतुर्वेदी (caturvedī).

چوتھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀉𑀢𑁆𑀣 (caüttha), from Sanskrit चतुर्थ (caturtha).

چوتھائی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀉𑀢𑁆𑀣 (caüttha) + ـائی (-āī).

چوٹ.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀘𑁄𑀝𑁆𑀝 (*coṭṭa), ultimately from Sanskrit चुट् (cuṭ, “to cut”).

چودنا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀘𑁄𑀤𑁆𑀤𑀢𑀺 (*coddati, “to copulate”), from Sanskrit चोदति (codati, “to incite”).

چودہ.e: Inherited from Old Hindi चौदह (caudaha), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀉𑀤𑁆𑀤𑀲 (caüddasa), from Sanskrit चतुर्दश (cáturdaśa).

چور.e: Inherited from Old Hindi چور (cvr /⁠cor⁠/) (c. 1392), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑁄𑀭 (cora), from Sanskrit चोर (corá), चौर (caurá). Compare Romani chor.

چوری.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑁄𑀭𑀻 (corī), from Sanskrit चौरिका (caurikā).

senses.g: breadth; the extent or measure of how broad or wide something is
the length of the longest path between two vertices on a graph

چوڑی.e: Feminine of چوڑا (cūṛā), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀽𑀟 (cūḍa), from Sanskrit चूड (cūḍa).

چوک.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀤𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓 (cadukka), from Sanskrit चतुष्क (catuṣka). Cognate with Bengali চক (cok), Punjabi چَوک (cauk) / ਚੌਕ (cauk), Nepali चोक (cok), Gujarati ચોક (cok), Odia ଚଉକ (cauka).

senses.g: any set of fours — a four
any set of fours — a four

چوکا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀉𑀓𑁆𑀓 (caükka), from Sanskrit चतुष्क (catuṣka).

چوگنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀘𑀉𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀼𑀡 (caügguṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit चतुर्गुण (cáturguṇa). First attested in 1639 as Middle Hindi چوگنا (cvgna /⁠caugunā⁠/). By surface analysis, چَوـ (cau-, “four”) + ـگُنا (-gunā, “-fold”).

چولھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀼𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀻 (cullī), 𑀘𑀼𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀺 (culli), from Sanskrit चुल्ली (cullī), borrowed from Dravidian. Compare Bengali চুলা (cula).

چومنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀼𑀁𑀩𑀤𑀺 (cuṃbadi), from Sanskrit चुम्बति (cumbati, “kisses”), from चुम्ब् (cumb), ultimately from Dravidian. Compare Tamil சூம்பு (cūmpu, “to suck, fondle with the lips”), Kumarbhag Paharia [script needed] (cumqe, “to kiss”).

چونا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀼𑀡𑁆𑀡 (cuṇṇa), from Sanskrit चूर्ण (cūrṇa, “lime; powder”).

senses.g: alternative form of چہ جائے کہ (cih jā'ye kih, “let alone”)

ex.u: یہ کہ انجیل کی تعلیم کامل بھی نہیں چہ جائے کہ اس کو بے نظیر رکھا جائے۔
ex.e: that the teaching of the Gospel is by no means complete, let alone deserving to be called incomparable.

چہ جائے کہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چه جای که (či jāy ki, “in the place of”).

چہار شنبہ.e: First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi چہار شنبہ (cahār śanba), borrowed from Classical Persian چهارشنبه (čahāršanba).

چہرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چِهْرَه (čihra, “face; visage”).

senses.g: Chechnya (a republic and federal subject of Russia, in the northern Caucasus)

چیخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جیغ (jīġ, “scream; squawk”).

senses.g: to form a path, cut a way

چیرنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀘𑀻𑀭𑁂𑀤𑀺 (*cīredi), from Sanskrit *चीरयति (*cīrayati, “to split”), verbalized from चीर (cī́ra, “strip of bark or cloth”).

چیز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian چیز (čīz, “object, thing, stuff”). Compare Punjabi ਚੀਜ਼ (cīz) / چِیز (cīz), Gujarati ચીજ (cīj), Marathi चीज (cīj), Bengali চিজ (cij).

senses.g: Czechoslovakia (a former country in Central Europe, now the Czech Republic and Slovakia)
Czech Republic, Czechia (a country in Central Europe)
Czech Republic, Czechia (a country in Central Europe)
kite (bird of prey of the family Accipitridae)

چیل.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀘𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀸 (cillā), from Sanskrit चिल्ला (cillā).

senses.g: Chillinji (a city in Ghizer district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)
comfort, tranquility, at rest, quiet, peace

چین.e: Inherited from Sanskrit शान्ति (śānti). Doublet of شانتی (śānti).

senses.g: a golden chain (a piece of jewellery)

چین.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit चीन (cīna). Possibly influenced by Classical Persian چین (čīn, “China”).

چھاپنا.e: Urdu verb set چَھپْنا (chapnā) چھاپْنا (chāpnā) چَھپْوَانا (chapvānā) Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀙𑀧𑁆𑀧𑁆 (*chapp) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

چھاؤنی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀸𑀬𑀡𑀻 (chāyaṇī), ultimately from Sanskrit छादन (chādana, “covering”). Cognate with Punjabi چَھوݨی (chauṇī), Sindhi ڇانوڻي (chānvaṇī) and Gujarati છાવણી (chāvṇī).

چھاؤں.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀙𑀸𑀬𑀸 (chāyā), from Sanskrit छाया (chāyā́), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *śćaHyáH, from Proto-Indo-European *(s)ḱeh₃ih₂. Cognate Punjabi چھان٘و (chāṉv).

چھبیس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀯𑁆𑀯𑀻𑀲𑀁 (chavvīsaṃ), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀲𑀟𑀼𑀯𑀻𑀲𑀢𑀺 (saḍuvīsati), from Sanskrit षड्विंशति (ṣaḍviṃśati).

چھپکلی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀺𑀧𑁆𑀧 (chippa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-) + -𑀮- (-la-) + -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit शेप्या (śepyā́, “tail”), a feminine form of शेप (śépa), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *šáypas. Cognate with Marathi शेपटी (śepṭī), शेपूट (śepūṭ), Konkani शेंपडी (śempḍī).

senses.g: passive of چھاپنا (chāpnā) — to be printed, to be stamped
passive of چھاپنا (chāpnā) — to be published

چھپنا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀙𑀼𑀧𑁆𑀧𑁆 (*chupp). See there for more.

ex.e: There are three fans on the ceiling.
There is someone standing on the roof.

چھت.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *छत्ति (*chatti, “covering, roof”), from the root छद् (chad, “to cover”) + -ति (-ti). Cognate to Punjabi ਛੱਤ (chatta) / چَھتّ (chatt).

چھتری.e: Inherited from Prakrit [Term?], from Sanskrit छत्र (chatra) / छत्त्र (chattra). Compare Hindi छाता (chātā), Nepali छातो (chāto), Punjabi چَھتْری (chatrī) / ਛਤਰੀ (chatrī).

چھٹا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀝 (chuṭṭa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit *क्षुट्ट (*kṣuṭṭa), from *क्षुट् (*kṣuṭ, “to release”, root) + -त (-ta, “-ed”). Compare چُھوٹْنَا (chūṭnā, “to be released”) and چُھوٹ (chūṭ, “liberty, freedom”). Cognate with Punjabi ਛੁੱਟ (chuṭṭa) / چُھٹّ (chuṭṭ), Gujarati છુટ્ટું (chuṭṭũ), Marathi सुटे (suṭe).

Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀝 (chuṭṭa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit *क्षुट्ट (*kṣuṭṭa), from *क्षुट् (*kṣuṭ, “to release”, root) + -त (-ta, “-ed”). Compare چُھوٹْنَا (chūṭnā, “to be released”) and چُھوٹ (chūṭ, “liberty, freedom”). Cognate with Punjabi ਛੁੱਟ (chuṭṭa) / چُھٹّ (chuṭṭ), Gujarati છુટ્ટું (chuṭṭũ), Marathi सुटे (suṭe).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀝𑁆𑀞 (chaṭṭha), from Sanskrit षष्ठ (ṣaṣṭha).

senses.g: to toss or tumble about, to flounder, to flutter

چھٹپٹانا.e: From چھٹپٹ (chaṭpaṭ) + آ (ā, causative augment) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

ex.u: اُس نے ایک ہَفْتے کی چُھٹّی مان٘گی۔
ex.r: us ne ek hafte kī chuṭṭī māṅgī.
ex.e: He asked for a week's leave.

چھٹی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀙𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀝 (chuṭṭa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-).

چھری.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀙𑀼𑀭𑀻 (churī), from Sanskrit क्षु॒र (kṣurá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *kšurás, from Proto-Indo-European *ksew- + *-rós.

چھڑکاؤ.e: From چھڑک (chiṛak) + ـاؤ (-āo, verbal noun suffix).

senses.g: any set of six — a six
any set of six — a six

چھکا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀓𑁆𑀓 (chakka) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit षट्क (ṣáṭka), but influenced by Northwestern Indo-Aryan, for which compare Punjabi چھکا (chakkā) and the other playing card numbers. *chākā would be the expected reflex in Urdu. Cognate with Nepali छक्का (chakkā).

senses.g: inflection of چُھوارا (chuvārā, “dried date”): — oblique/vocative singular
inflection of چُھوارا (chuvārā, “dried date”): — direct plural

چھوٹ.e: From the stem of چُھوٹْنَا (chūṭnā, “to be released”).

ex.u: باہَر ایک چھوٹا بَچَّہ کَھڑا ہَے۔
ex.r: bāhar ek choṭā baccā khaṛā hai.
ex.e: There's a small child standing outside.

senses.g: trivial, trifling, unimportant, of no consequence

چھوٹا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀙𑁄𑀝𑁆𑀝 (*choṭṭa). Compare Nepali छोटो (choṭo, “small”), Gujarati છોટું (choṭũ), Punjabi ਛੋਟਾ (choṭā, “small”), Odia ଛୋଟ (choṭa, “small, lesser”), Assamese ছোট (süto, “junior, low grade”). Earlier lexicographers suggested Sanskrit क्षुद्रक (kṣudraka, “small, insignificant”).

senses.g: to be given up; to be dismissed, fired

چھوٹنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀙𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀤𑀺 (*chuṭṭadi), from Sanskrit *क्षुट्यते (*kṣuṭyate), from *क्षुट् (*kṣuṭ, “to release”, root).

senses.g: to drop (ie. drop off or drop on the floor)
to leave (ie. leave behind items or reminiscence)

چھونا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀙𑀼𑀯𑀇 (chuvaï). Cognate with Punjabi ਛੂਹਣਾ (chūhṇā) / چھوہنا (chūhṇā).

senses.g: Chongqing (a direct-administered municipality and city in western China)

ex.u: چھونگ چھنگ (شِنہوا) چین کی جنوب مغربی چھونگ چھنگ میونسپلٹی کی بیلٹ اینڈ روڈ کے گرد ممالک اور خطوں کے ساتھ تجارت میں 2019 کے دوران 3 فیصد سالانہ کا اضافہ ہوا جو میونسپلٹی کی کل تجارت کا 27 فیصد ہے۔
ex.r: choṅg chiṅg (śinhūā) cīn kī junūb maġribī choṅg chiṅg miyūnisipaliṭī kī bailṭ ainḍ roḍ ke gird mumālik aur xit̤t̤oṉ ke sāth tijārat meṉ 2019 ke daurān 3 fīṣad sālāna kā iẓāfa hūā jo miyūnisipaliṭī kī kul tijārat kā 27 fīṣad hai.
ex.e: Chongqing (Xinhua) China's southwestern Chongqing municipality's trade with countries and regions along the Belt and Road increased by 3 percent annually in 2019, accounting for 27 percent of the municipality's total trade.

چھونگ چھنگ.e: Borrowed from Mandarin 重慶／重庆 (chóngqìng).

چھہ.e: From Sanskrit षष् (ṣaṣ), from Proto-Indo-Iranian, from Proto-Indo-European *swéḱs.

چھیلا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀙𑀯𑀺 (chavi) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀮𑁆𑀮- (-lla-), from Sanskrit छवी (chavī́). Cognate with Odia ଛଇଳା (chaiḷā), Punjabi ਛੈਲਾ /چھَیلا (chailā).

چھینک.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀙𑀺𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸 (chikkā), from Sanskrit छिक्का (chikkā, onomatopoeia).

چھیننا.e: First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi چھین (chīn), inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀙𑀺𑀡𑁆𑀡 (chiṇṇa), from Sanskrit छिन्न (chinna).

senses.g: The ninth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by چ and followed by خ.
a want, need, necessity, exigency, requirement
call of nature, needing to use the lavatory

حاجت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حاجت (hājat), from Arabic حَاجَة (ḥāja).

senses.g: a place where wants and needs are fulfilled, a temple or place of worship

حادثہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَادِثَه (hādisa), from Arabic حَادِثَة (ḥādiṯa).

حاصل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَاصِل (hāsil), from Arabic حَاصِل (ḥāṣil).

senses.g: Hasilpur (a city in Bahawalpur district, Punjab, Pakistan)

حاضر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حاضر (hāzir), from Arabic حَاضِر (ḥāḍir).

حاضری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حاضری (hāzirī), derived from Arabic حَاضِر (ḥāḍir). By surface analysis, حاضر (hāzir) + ی (-ī).

حافظ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حافظ (hāfiz), from Arabic حَافِظ (ḥāfiẓ), from the verb حَفِظَ (ḥafiẓa, “to preserve, to protect, to defend”).

senses.g: hafiz (one who knows the Qur'an by heart)

حافظ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حافظ (hāfiz), from Arabic حَافِظ (ḥāfiẓ), from the verb حَفِظَ (ḥafiẓa, “to preserve, to protect, to defend”).

حافظہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَافِظَه (hāfiza), from Arabic حَافِظَة (ḥāfiẓa).

حال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَال (hāl), borrowed from Arabic حَال (ḥāl).

senses.g: now, at this moment, at present

حالانکہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حالا که (hālā ke).

حالت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حالت (hālat), from Arabic حَالَة (ḥāla).

senses.g: recent (having happened a short while ago)

ex.u: یہ تو اب ثابت ہوگیا کہ حالیہ الیکشن میں تابڑ توڑ دھاندلی ہوئی تھی
ex.r: Ye to ab sābit hogayā ke hāliya ilekšan me tābaṛtoṛ dhāndhlī huī thī.
ex.e: It has now been proven that there was rampant rigging in the recent election.

حامی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَامِی (hāmī), borrowed from Arabic حامٍ (ḥāmin). First attested in c. 1672 as Middle Hindi حامی (ḥamy /⁠hāmī⁠/).

senses.g: A person from the lineage of Hām bin Nūh
A group of African languages including Egyptian and the Berber languages
hindering, standing in the way, disturbing

حائل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حائل (hā'il), from Arabic حَائِل (ḥāʔil).
Borrowed from Classical Persian حائل (hā'il), from Arabic حَائِل (ḥāʔil).

senses.g: Hub (a city in Hub district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
Abyssinia (former name of Ethiopia: a country and former empire in East Africa; used as an exonym until the mid 20th century)

حبش.e: Borrowed from Arabic حَبَشْ (ḥabaš, “Abyssinia”), the former name of Ethiopia.

حبشہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حبشه (habša), from Arabic الحَبْشَة (al-ḥabša).

حبشی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حبشی (habašī, “Abyssinian; Negro; black slave”), from Arabic حَبَشِيّ (ḥabašiyy).

حبقوق.e: Borrowed from Arabic حَبَقُّوق (ḥabaqqūq), from Hebrew חֲבַקּוּק (khavakúk).

حج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَجّ (hajj), from Arabic حَجّ (ḥajj).

حجام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَجَّام (hajjām), borrowed from Arabic حَجَّام (ḥajjām).
